comments.text: A lot about these questions can be deduced from the work of Pollack–Pomerance–Treviño. Here's the note written with GPT-5.4 Pro, with full or partial answers to the above. (I don't claim completeness, but it should be close).

Even though [ErGr, p. 82] does claim that $F(n) \asymp \log\log\log n$, it is not clear to me where this bound is supposed to come from. I can't immediately spot it in [Er36b] in any case, as the latter paper only directly seems to deal with comparing consecutive values. A quick check with ChatGPT also didn't locate a source.

Hmm, I agree. In that paper Erdős proves that that the number of $m\leq n$ such that $\phi(m+1)> \phi(m)$ is $\sim n/2$, and similarly for $\phi(m+1)< \phi(m)$. It may be that the method naturally generalises to give the claim for longer inequality patterns, but this is not immediately obvious to me.

Congrats. I ran standard check which found 1 issue related to mismatch of Proposition 3.3 and Theorem 1.2. Perhaps easy to repair, but I think checking is less confident for a writeup with a lot of implicit bookkeeping - e.g. Proposition 3.3's "For the integer $n$ constructed above...".
